statement.p: Let x^{15}\cdot x^{a}=x^{30}\text{,} where a is a natural number. How many possibilities are there for a\text{?}

solution.p: Let x^{15}\cdot x^{a}=x^{30}\text{.} Then a must be 30-15\text{.} So there is only one possibility for a\text{.}

statement.p: Let x^{b}\cdot x^{c}=x^{70}\text{,} where b and c are natural numbers. How many possibilities are there for b\text{?}

solution.p: Let x^{b}\cdot x^{c}=x^{70}\text{.} Many possibilities exist for b and c\text{.} Since b and c must be natural numbers, lets start with b equal to 1\text{.} Then c must equal 69, because x^{1}\cdot x^{69}=x^{70}\text{.} Considering all of the possibilities for b\text{,} notice that b can equal 1,2,3,\ldots,69\text{.} Therefore, there are 69 possibilities for b\text{.} Note that b cant equal 70 because that would force c to be zero,which is not a natural number.

statement.p: Let x^{d}\cdot x^{e}=x^{1950} where d and e are natural numbers. How many possibilities are there for d\text{?}

solution.p: Let x^{d}\cdot x^{e}=x^{1950}\text{.} Many possibilities exist for d and e\text{.} Since d and e must be natural numbers, lets start with d equal to 1\text{.} Then e must equal 1949, because x^{1}\cdot x^{1949}=x^{1950}\text{.} Considering all of the possibilities for d\text{,} notice that d can equal 1,2,3,\ldots,1949\text{.} Therefore, there are 1949 possibilities for d\text{.} Note that d cant equal 1950 because that would force e to be zero, which is not a natural number.
